name: Hamilton Quarry
short_description: A historic quarry site in northwestern Illinois offering exposures of Paleozoic sedimentary rocks with quartz veins, feldspathic minerals, and calcite crystal specimens.
description:
Hamilton Quarry sits in Hancock County in the Upper Mississippi Valley region, an area rich in Paleozoic-age bedrock and geological interest. The quarry exposes sedimentary formations from the Ordovician and Silurian periods, when shallow seas covered much of Illinois. The gentle slope terrain makes this a moderately accessible site for rockhounders interested in crystalline minerals and rock specimens.

The exposed quarry walls reveal quartz veins cutting through feldspathic host rock, with calcite crystals common in fractures and vugs. Chert nodules are also present in the surrounding sedimentary layers. The site offers excellent opportunities to study mineral-bearing rock formations typical of the Mississippi Valley geology.

Collectors should expect to find small to medium quartz points, translucent to white calcite crystal clusters, and fragments of feldspar-rich rock. The quarry setting means stable, accessible rock faces with relatively fresh exposures compared to weathered surface deposits. Water percolation through the bedrock has enhanced crystal development in many specimens.

Access and current activity status of this quarry should be verified before visiting, as quarry operations and property status can change. Always confirm with local authorities or property owners regarding collection permissions and site safety.
history: Hamilton Quarry developed in the heart of the Upper Mississippi Valley, an area extensively quarried for building stone and aggregate throughout the 19th and 20th centuries. The surrounding bedrock consists of Ordovician and Silurian carbonate and siliciclastic sequences deposited during marine transgressions across the North American platform, with quartz and feldspar concentrated in veins and primary mineral assemblages formed during diagenesis and later subsurface alteration.
tips: Search the quarry walls and rubble piles at the base of fresh exposures where weathering has loosened mineral specimens. Bring a small hammer and chisel to carefully extract calcite crystals from vugs in the exposed rock face, and look for quartz veins running through the feldspathic country rock. Examine fracture surfaces for transparent to translucent specimens; the best quality crystals often form in open spaces where mineral-rich groundwater could flow freely.
terrain_notes: Elevation 416 feet on gentle slope. The quarry setting features exposed rock walls, rubble piles, and relatively even terrain underfoot with scattered angular rock fragments. Walking distance from parking varies depending on access point; expect stable footing on quarry floor and slopes between 0-15 degrees. No significant vegetation interference.
best_season: April - October. Late spring through early fall offers the most comfortable rockhounding conditions in Illinois, with moderate temperatures and lower precipitation. Winter freeze-thaw cycles can destabilize loose material, and spring flooding may limit access to some areas.
written_directions: From Hancock County, contact local authorities or property owner to confirm current access and directions to Hamilton Quarry. The site is located at approximately 40.382197, -91.355437 in northwestern Illinois. Standard passenger vehicles may access the general area via county roads, but specific quarry access depends on current operational status and property permissions.
road_conditions: Likely unpaved quarry roads; conditions vary with maintenance and seasonal weather.
parking_notes: Limited parking at quarry site; expect informal pullout areas. Capacity and exact location subject to current site conditions and operational status.
collection_rules: Collection requires explicit permission from property owner or current quarry operator. No commercial use allowed. Respect all private property boundaries and quarry safety regulations. Do not collect from active mining areas. Verify current access status and obtain written permission before visiting.
rules: Obtain property owner permission before collecting. No commercial collection. Respect quarry safety protocols. No collecting from active work zones. Property access subject to change.
nearest_services: Hancock County area (distance varies); nearest significant town approximately 10-15 miles for gas, food, and water.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky ground, scattered angular fragments, and stable quarry floor. Low-slope exposure makes site reasonably accessible to most ability levels, but loose rock requires careful footing. Sturdy footwear essential. Not wheelchair accessible.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Hamilton Quarry in Illinois?
faq.answer: Hamilton Quarry yields quartz points and veins, white to clear calcite crystal clusters, feldspar-rich rock specimens, and chert nodules. The best specimens are typically found in vugs (small cavities) within the exposed quarry walls where crystals formed in protected spaces.

faq.question: Is Hamilton Quarry open to the public for rockhounding near Hancock County?
faq.answer: Hamilton Quarry is private property and requires explicit permission from the owner or current operator before collecting. Contact Hancock County authorities to identify the current property holder and verify access status, as quarry operations and ownership can change.

faq.question: What tools do you need for rockhounding at Illinois quarries like Hamilton?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer, cold chisel, and pry bar to extract specimens from the quarry walls and rubble. A hand lens helps identify mineral types in the field, and a sturdy backpack lets you safely carry quartz and calcite specimens. Wear heavy gloves and eye protection.

faq.question: What is the best season to visit Hamilton Quarry for crystal hunting?
faq.answer: Visit April through October for the most comfortable rockhounding conditions in Illinois. These months offer moderate temperatures and lower precipitation. Avoid winter freeze-thaw cycles, which can destabilize loose quarry material, and early spring flooding.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Hamilton Quarry for beginners?
faq.answer: The gentle terrain and stable quarry floor make Hamilton suitable for beginners willing to follow safety rules. The mineral specimens found here (quartz, calcite, feldspar) are clearly visible and relatively easy to extract. Always wear proper protective equipment and confirm access before visiting.

faq.question: What is the geology behind the minerals found at Hamilton Quarry?
faq.answer: Hamilton Quarry exposes Ordovician and Silurian bedrock from when shallow seas covered Illinois. Quartz veins and feldspar formed during diagenesis and subsurface alteration, while calcite crystals developed later in fractures and vugs as mineral-rich groundwater percolated through the rock.

meta_description: Find quartz, calcite, and feldspar at Hamilton Quarry in Hancock County, Illinois. Private property requires owner permission. Moderate difficulty.
